gettingStarted.title: Getting Started
gettingStarted.subtitle: Welcome to the @uu-webtools/client documentation. Learn how to integrate translations and error tracking into your application.
gettingStarted.overviewDesc: @uu-webtools/client is an npm package that provides two main modules for your Next.js or React application:
gettingStarted.overviewItem1: i18n Module — file-based translations with cookie-based locale switching and missing translation reporting
gettingStarted.overviewItem2: Error Module — automatic error capture, reporting, and error boundaries
gettingStarted.overviewItem3: CLI Tool — pull and push translations between your project and the Web Tools dashboard
gettingStarted.overviewItem4: Full TypeScript support with server and client entry points
gettingStarted.installationDesc: Install the package using npm or yarn:
gettingStarted.authDesc: Learn how to authenticate your requests using project tokens
gettingStarted.i18nDesc: Set up translations, locale switching, and missing translation reporting
gettingStarted.errorDesc: Capture and report errors automatically from your application
gettingStarted.cliDesc: Sync translations between your local files and the Web Tools dashboard
gettingStarted.prerequisitesDesc: Before you begin, ensure you have:
gettingStarted.prerequisiteItem1: An active Web Tools account with a project created
gettingStarted.prerequisiteItem2: Your project token from the project settings page

authentication.title: Authentication
authentication.subtitle: Access to the Web Tools API requires a project token.
authentication.getTokenInSettings: You can create or view your project token in the Project Settings page of the Athena dashboard.
authentication.requireToken: The project token is sent via the X-Project-Token header. Without it, or with an invalid token, API requests will fail with 401. When using the SDK, set the NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBTOOLS_PROJECT_TOKEN environment variable and the token is sent automatically.
authentication.error401Missing: Token missing — the X-Project-Token header was not sent.
authentication.error401Invalid: Invalid or unrecognized token — the token does not match any project.

errorHandling.title: Error Module
errorHandling.subtitle: Capture and report errors from your application to the Web Tools dashboard.
errorHandling.overviewDesc: The error module provides automatic error tracking for your application. It captures errors, sends them to the Web Tools API, and displays them on your project dashboard.
errorHandling.overviewItem1: Automatic capture of unhandled errors and promise rejections
errorHandling.overviewItem4: Custom tags and extra metadata on each error report
errorHandling.installationDesc: The error module is included in the @uu-webtools/client package:
errorHandling.environmentSetupDesc: Add your project token to your environment variables. The SDK reads this automatically — you don't need to pass it as a prop:
errorHandling.environmentSetupNote: The token is optional as a prop. If not provided, the SDK falls back to the NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBTOOLS_PROJECT_TOKEN environment variable.
errorHandling.basicSetupDesc: Wrap your application with the NextErrorProvider component in your root layout. This sets up both error capturing and the error boundary:
errorHandling.basicSetupAuto1: Initializes the error client with your project token
errorHandling.basicSetupAuto2: Installs global handlers for uncaught errors and unhandled promise rejections
errorHandling.basicSetupAuto3: Wraps children in an ErrorBoundary that catches React render errors
errorHandling.advancedConfigDesc: You can customize the error provider with additional options:
errorHandling.advancedConfigToken: All props are optional. The projectToken defaults to the NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBTOOLS_PROJECT_TOKEN environment variable.
errorHandling.manualReportingDesc: Use captureException() to manually report errors, for example in try/catch blocks or API error handlers:
errorHandling.errorBoundaryDesc: You can also use the ErrorBoundary component directly to wrap specific parts of your app with custom fallback UI:
errorHandling.errorBoundaryHow: When a React render error occurs inside the boundary, it automatically reports the error via captureException() and displays the fallback UI.
errorHandling.viewingErrorsDesc: All reported errors appear in your project dashboard on Athena:
errorHandling.viewingItem1: Navigate to your project in the Athena dashboard
errorHandling.viewingItem2: The Errors section shows all captured errors with timestamps
errorHandling.viewingItem3: View error details including stack traces, tags, and metadata
errorHandling.viewingItem4: Delete individual errors or all duplicates from the action menu

i18nPage.title: i18n Module
i18nPage.subtitle: File-based internationalization with cookie-based locale switching for Next.js and React.
i18nPage.overviewDesc: The i18n module provides a complete translation system for your application:
i18nPage.overviewItem2: Cookie-based locale persistence across server and client
i18nPage.overviewItem3: useTranslation hook with optional prefix support
i18nPage.overviewItem4: Automatic missing translation reporting to the Web Tools dashboard
i18nPage.installationDesc: The i18n module is included in the @uu-webtools/client package:
i18nPage.fileStructureDesc: Create a messages directory in your project root with one folder per locale. Each folder contains a common.json file with your translations:
i18nPage.serverSetupDesc: In your root layout (server component), load messages using the server entry point and pass them to the I18nProvider. The locale is read from a cookie:
i18nPage.useTranslationDesc: Use the useTranslation hook in client components to access translations. It returns an object with t (translate function), locale, and changeLocale:
i18nPage.useTranslationPrefix: The optional prefix parameter lets you scope translations to avoid repeating the full key path:
i18nPage.languageSwitchingDesc: The changeLocale function returned by useTranslation sets a cookie and triggers a hard page reload so the server layout re-reads the locale:
i18nPage.languageSwitchingHow: changeLocale() sets the webtools-locale cookie (valid for 1 year) and reloads the page. The server layout reads this cookie via LOCALE_COOKIE_NAME to determine the active locale.
i18nPage.missingTranslationsDesc: When a translation key is not found, the module automatically:
i18nPage.missingItem1: Logs a console error with the missing key and locale
i18nPage.missingItem2: Sends a POST request to the Web Tools error API with the missing key details
i18nPage.missingItem3: Returns the raw key as fallback text so the UI doesn't break
i18nPage.missingTranslationsCustom: You can also provide a custom handler via the onMissingTranslation prop on I18nProvider:
i18nPage.syncWithCliDesc: Use the @uu-webtools/cli to sync translations between your local files and the Web Tools dashboard. For full documentation, see the
i18nPage.automationDesc: You can automatically pull the latest translations before running your dev server or building your app by adding pre-scripts to your package.json:
i18nPage.automationHow: npm automatically runs these scripts before the corresponding command:
i18nPage.automationPredev: runs before npm run dev — always start with fresh translations
i18nPage.automationPrebuild: runs before npm run build — ensures production builds have the latest translations
i18nPage.automationMore: For more details about the CLI, see the
i18nPage.serverExportsDesc: The server entry point (@uu-webtools/client/server) provides utilities for loading messages in server components. These must not be imported in client components:

cli.title: CLI Tool
cli.subtitle: Sync translations between your local project and the Web Tools dashboard from the command line.
cli.overviewDesc: The @uu-webtools/cli package provides a set of commands to manage your translations workflow:
cli.overviewItem1: Initialize your project with the correct file structure and environment variables
cli.overviewItem2: Pull translations from the Web Tools dashboard to your local messages/ directory
cli.overviewItem3: Push local translations back to the dashboard for team collaboration
cli.overviewItem4: Check the status of your project's languages and translation coverage
cli.overviewI18nLink: The CLI works with the file structure used by the
cli.installationDesc: Install the CLI globally or use it via npx:
cli.initDesc: Initialize Web Tools in your project. This is the first command you should run when setting up a new project:
cli.initItem1: Create a messages/ directory for your translation files
cli.initItem2: Create or update .env.local with the NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBTOOLS_PROJECT_TOKEN variable
cli.initItem3: Automatically pull translations if a valid token is already set
cli.pullDesc: Download translations from the Web Tools dashboard and save them to your local messages/ directory:
cli.pushDesc: Upload your local translation files to the Web Tools dashboard:
cli.pushHow: This reads all JSON files from your messages/ directory and uploads them. The dashboard will show created and updated counts for each language.
cli.statusDesc: Show the current status of your project's translations:
cli.statusHow: Displays the number of languages configured and the key count for each locale. Warns about languages that have no translations yet.
cli.environmentDesc: The CLI requires a project token to authenticate with the Web Tools API. Set it in your environment file:
cli.environmentLookup: The CLI looks for the token in this order:
cli.environmentTokenLink: You can generate a token in the
cli.automationDesc: Automate translation syncing by adding npm pre-scripts to your package.json. This ensures your app always starts with the latest translations:
cli.automationHow: npm automatically runs pre-scripts before the corresponding command:
cli.automationPredev: runs before npm run dev — always start development with fresh translations
cli.automationPrebuild: runs before npm run build — ensures production builds include the latest translations
cli.automationTip: This is the recommended setup for teams. Every developer will always have up-to-date translations without having to manually run the CLI.
cli.fileStructureDesc: The CLI expects and creates the following file structure in your project:
